mnemonics.title: Buffer Overflow Prevention
mnemonics.content: SAND: Stack canaries, ASLR, Non-executable memory, DEP - combined defenses against overflow attacks

mnemonics.title: Safe String Functions
mnemonics.content: SAFE: strcpy_s, strncpy_s, strcat_s, sprintf_s - always specify buffer size

mnemonics.title: Secure Coding Rules
mnemonics.content: NOPE: Never use gets(), Always check bounds, Prefer safe libraries, Enable compiler warnings

acronyms.usage: Randomizes memory addresses to make exploits unpredictable
Prevents code execution in non-code memory regions
CPU bit that marks memory pages as non-executable
Exploit technique using existing code fragments to bypass DEP
Enables ASLR for the main executable itself
Windows exception handling mechanism targeted by exploits

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a row of mailboxes (stack frames), where overflowing one mailbox (buffer) spills over into the next (return address), changing delivery destination
Think of library locations as random parking spots each time you restart - attacker can't find the code to jump to
Place a 'tripwire' (canary value) between your valuable items (return address) and the door (buffer); if tampered, alarm sounds
Hotel rooms (heap blocks) with shared walls; overflow in one room can corrupt the registry (metadata) of neighboring rooms

analogies.analogy: Like filling a glass (buffer) beyond its capacity - water overflows and spills onto surrounding items (adjacent memory)
Like a skeleton key that opens all doors - once executed, gives attacker complete control of the system
Like a 'No Entry' sign on a document - even if someone writes instructions on paper (data), it can't be executed as commands
Like assembling a ransom note from letters cut out of different magazines - uses existing legitimate code pieces to form malicious instructions
